title: The Peril of Cymbaline Isle - Session 2 (Solo RPG using Scarlet Heroes, White Box, and more)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lTCDfNnY4u8

Daniel (@dungeondive) leans into the warts-and-all framing and uses session two to populate the Crooked Violin with a regular cast — Reya the half-Shou shaman, Porthus the wise old warrior, his halfling-barbarian companion Pip, Shanthala the elven ranger, and Brook the haunted dwarven miner — names sourced from channel patrons. The tavern's trapdoor stays covered for now but is openly flagged as a future thread.

The investigation into the Plundered Tribute hook drives the session. Harlan and Xavier track down Saleem Sikaway — the priest, Xavier's old friend — and find him being shaken down at his home by two cult thugs. Combat is run with Scarlet Heroes' Fray die and the abstracted range tracker from Ancient Odysseys, demonstrating how the system telescopes encounters for a single hero. Harlan wins, beats the cult's name out of the survivors — Slaves of the Judging Cloud — and banks his first victory point and clue.

Daniel surfaces the broader design point: the urban adventure system gives him a game scaffold (scenes, victory points, heat, threat) over the Fiasco-style improvisation, and he argues that scaffolding is what makes solo play feel like a game rather than make-believe. Table Fables earns a name-check as the most-used solo accessory on YouTube.

----

Is the explicit scene/victory-point structure of Scarlet Heroes' urban mode a help or a hindrance for solo play that wants to feel like fiction?
